unit.title: What are the mechanisms of chemical change?

title: Electron-pair sharing reactions
summary: A huge fraction of organic chemistry comes down to one idea: a pair of electrons moving from somewhere electron-rich to somewhere electron-poor, drawn as a curly arrow. This lesson builds R3.4 from that single move. We define nucleophiles (electron-pair donors) and electrophiles (electron-pair acceptors), then work through the two reaction families the syllabus asks for — nucleophilic substitution of halogenoalkanes (e.g. with OH⁻) and electrophilic addition to alkenes (e.g. with Br₂ or HBr), including Markovnikov's rule. HL material — the SN1 and SN2 mechanisms and the detailed addition mechanisms — is flagged clearly so SL students know exactly where their syllabus stops. Every worked example is laid out the way our marking engine awards points, with M (method), A (answer) and ECF made explicit.
learningObjectives: Explain what a curly (double-headed) arrow represents and where it must start and end.
Define nucleophile (electron-pair donor) and electrophile (electron-pair acceptor) and identify each in a reaction.
State the overall equation for the nucleophilic substitution of a halogenoalkane by a nucleophile such as OH⁻, and identify the δ+ carbon the nucleophile attacks.
State the overall equation and product for the electrophilic addition of Br₂ or HBr to an alkene, and apply Markovnikov's rule to predict the major product.
(HL) Distinguish SN1 and SN2 mechanisms for primary and tertiary halogenoalkanes and relate them to rate, and describe the detailed electrophilic addition mechanism.
Present reasoning so that method marks and error-carried-forward (ECF) protect the marks that follow.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL; SN1/SN2 mechanisms and detailed addition mechanisms are HL.

simpleExplanation.title: Follow the Electron Pair
simpleExplanation.summary: Almost every organic reaction is a story about one pair of electrons deciding to move. A curly arrow tells that story: it always begins at a lump of electrons (a lone pair or a bond) and points to the atom that is short of them. Learn to read that arrow and you can predict what attacks what, and what the product is.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a busy dance floor. A nucleophile is a dancer holding a spare pair of hands (a lone pair) looking for a partner; an electrophile is a dancer standing alone with an empty, positive-feeling spot. The curly arrow is the hand reaching out — always from the one with spare hands to the one who is short. In substitution, the newcomer taps in and the old partner (the leaving group) walks off; in addition, a double-bond couple opens up so two newcomers can join, and nobody leaves.
steps: Find the electron-rich site (a lone pair or a C=C double bond) — that is where an arrow starts.
Find the electron-poor site (a δ+ carbon, or an incoming δ+/positive atom) — that is where an arrow points.
Decide the family: a halogenoalkane losing a leaving group is substitution; an alkene gaining atoms across the double bond is addition.
Write the overall equation and read off the organic product; then, if HL, add the mechanism arrows and any intermediate.

flashcards.back: A symbol for the movement of a PAIR of electrons. It must start at a source of electrons (a lone pair or a bond) and its head must point to the atom or bond that receives them. Never start it at a positive charge.

An electron-rich species that DONATES a pair of electrons to form a new covalent bond. It is a negative ion or a neutral molecule with a lone pair, e.g. OH⁻, CN⁻, NH₃, H₂O. 'Nucleus-loving' — attracted to δ+ centres.

An electron-deficient species that ACCEPTS a pair of electrons to form a new covalent bond. A positive ion or a molecule with a δ+ atom, e.g. H⁺, the δ+ carbon in C–Br, or the polarised Br₂ approaching a double bond. 'Electron-loving'.
A reaction in which a nucleophile replaces a leaving group on a saturated carbon. General: R–X + Nu⁻ → R–Nu + X⁻. The nucleophile attacks the δ+ carbon; the halide leaves as X⁻.

A reaction in which a molecule adds across a C=C double bond, which is broken and replaced by two new single bonds. Nothing leaves — every atom of both reactants ends up in one product. General: C=C + XY → X–C–C–Y.

flashcards.front: The δ+ carbon in a halogenoalkane
flashcards.back: The carbon bonded to the halogen. Because the halogen is more electronegative, the C–X bond is polar (Cᵟ⁺–Xᵟ⁻), so that carbon is electron-poor and is the site the nucleophile attacks.
The atom or group that departs taking the bonding pair with it, e.g. Br⁻ from bromoethane. In halogenoalkanes the halide ion is the leaving group; C–I breaks most easily, C–F least.

When an unsymmetrical molecule such as HBr adds to an unsymmetrical alkene, the H attaches to the carbon that already has more H atoms, so the halogen ends up on the more substituted carbon. This gives the MAJOR product.

A species with a positively charged, electron-deficient carbon (R₃C⁺). It is an electrophile and appears as an intermediate in electrophilic addition and in the HL SN1 mechanism. Stability: tertiary > secondary > primary.

Substitution, Nucleophilic, bimolecular. One concerted step: the nucleophile attacks the δ+ carbon as the leaving group departs. Rate depends on BOTH species. Favoured by PRIMARY halogenoalkanes (little steric hindrance).

Substitution, Nucleophilic, unimolecular. Two steps: the C–X bond breaks first to give a carbocation (slow, rate-determining), then the nucleophile attacks. Rate depends only on the halogenoalkane. Favoured by TERTIARY halogenoalkanes (stable carbocation).

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: which way does the arrow go?
flashcards.back: A curly arrow ALWAYS goes from electron-rich to electron-poor: lone pair → δ+ carbon, or bonding pair → atom that keeps it. Drawing it from the positive carbon towards the nucleophile is a guaranteed lost mark.

quickCheck.prompt: In the reaction CH₃CH₂Br + OH⁻ → CH₃CH₂OH + Br⁻, the hydroxide ion is acting as the:
options: Electrophile, because it accepts electrons
Nucleophile, because it donates a lone pair to the δ+ carbon
Leaving group, because it departs from the molecule
Free radical, because it has an unpaired electron
quickCheck.answer: Nucleophile, because it donates a lone pair to the δ+ carbon

quickCheck.prompt: Which carbon does the nucleophile attack in 2-bromobutane, CH₃CHBrCH₂CH₃?
options: Any of the four carbons equally
The carbon bonded to Br, because it carries the δ+ charge
The terminal CH₃ carbon furthest from Br
The bromine atom, not a carbon
quickCheck.answer: The carbon bonded to Br, because it carries the δ+ charge

quickCheck.prompt: Ethene reacts with bromine water. This reaction is best classified as:
options: Nucleophilic substitution
Electrophilic addition
Homolytic (radical) substitution
Acid–base neutralisation

quickCheck.prompt: When HBr adds to propene (CH₃CH=CH₂), Markovnikov's rule predicts the MAJOR product is:
options: 1-bromopropane, CH₃CH₂CH₂Br
2-bromopropane, CH₃CHBrCH₃
1,2-dibromopropane
propan-2-ol

quickCheck.prompt: (HL) A tertiary halogenoalkane such as 2-bromo-2-methylpropane undergoes hydrolysis mainly by:
options: SN2, because the nucleophile attacks in one concerted step
SN1, because it forms a relatively stable tertiary carbocation intermediate
Electrophilic addition, because it has a δ+ carbon
SN1 and SN2 at exactly equal rates
quickCheck.answer: SN1, because it forms a relatively stable tertiary carbocation intermediate

faq.q: How do I tell a nucleophile from an electrophile quickly?
faq.a: Ask one question: does the species give away a pair of electrons or take one? A nucleophile GIVES (donates) a lone pair — it is electron-rich, usually a negative ion or a molecule with a lone pair such as OH⁻, CN⁻, NH₃ or H₂O. An electrophile TAKES (accepts) a pair — it is electron-poor, a positive ion or a δ+ atom such as H⁺ or the carbon of a C–Br bond. The '-phile' ending tells you what it loves, not what it is: a nucleophile loves the (positive) nucleus, an electrophile loves electrons.

faq.q: Why does the nucleophile attack the carbon and not the halogen in a halogenoalkane?
faq.a: The C–X bond is polar because the halogen is more electronegative, so the shared electrons sit closer to the halogen. That leaves the CARBON short of electrons (δ+) and the halogen electron-rich (δ−). A nucleophile is attracted to the electron-poor site, so it targets the δ+ carbon. The halogen then leaves as a stable halide ion, taking the bonding pair with it — it is the leaving group, not the target.

faq.q: What is the difference between substitution and addition in one line?
faq.a: In substitution something is swapped: a group leaves and a new one takes its place (R–X + Nu⁻ → R–Nu + X⁻), so there are two products. In addition nothing leaves: a double bond opens and two atoms add across it to make a single product (C=C + XY → X–C–C–Y). Halogenoalkanes (saturated, with a leaving group) do substitution; alkenes (unsaturated, with a C=C) do addition.

faq.q: Do I need the SN1/SN2 mechanisms and the detailed addition mechanism at SL?
faq.a: No. At SL you need the overall equations, the classification (nucleophilic substitution / electrophilic addition), the identity of the nucleophile and the δ+ carbon it attacks, the product (including the Markovnikov major product), and what a curly arrow represents. The step-by-step SN1 vs SN2 comparison, the rate dependence, and the full curly-arrow addition mechanism with a carbocation intermediate are HL only. This lesson flags every HL passage so you can see exactly where the SL syllabus ends.

sections.content: Organic molecules look complicated, but the way they react is governed by a single, repeating event: a pair of electrons moving from a place that has plenty to a place that is short. Chemists draw that movement as a curly arrow, and once you can read it, most of organic reactivity falls into a handful of patterns. This lesson takes the two patterns the R3.4 syllabus asks for — nucleophilic substitution of halogenoalkanes and electrophilic addition to alkenes — and builds them from that one idea. Every worked example is laid out the way our marking engine awards the marks, because in structured Paper 2 answers HOW you justify each step decides how many marks you keep.
The one move: a curly arrow

A covalent bond is a shared pair of electrons, so making and breaking bonds is about where electron pairs go. A curly (double-headed) arrow shows the movement of a pair of electrons. It has two non-negotiable rules: it must START at a source of electrons — a lone pair on an atom, or the pair of electrons in a bond — and its head must POINT to the atom or bond that receives them. An arrow tail on a positive charge, or an arrow drawn from the electron-poor atom towards the electron-rich one, is chemically wrong and loses marks. In the reactions below, one arrow makes a new bond and another breaks an old one.

items: A curly arrow = the movement of a PAIR of electrons (a single-barbed 'fish-hook' arrow, for a single electron, belongs to radical reactions and is not needed here).
Tail: on a lone pair or a bond. Head: on the atom/bond that gains the electrons.
Electrons flow from electron-RICH to electron-POOR — never the other way.
Making a bond and breaking a bond are usually shown by two separate arrows.

sections.content: The two players: nucleophiles and electrophiles

Every electron-pair reaction has a donor and an acceptor. A nucleophile is an electron-rich species that DONATES a pair of electrons to form a new bond; it is a negative ion or a neutral molecule carrying a lone pair. An electrophile is an electron-deficient species that ACCEPTS a pair of electrons to form a new bond; it is a positive ion or a molecule with a δ+ atom. These are the same species that acid–base theory calls Lewis bases and Lewis acids — the word 'nucleophile' or 'electrophile' is used when we are talking about a reaction mechanism rather than a proton transfer.

items: **Nucleophiles** (electron-pair donors): OH⁻, CN⁻, H₂O, NH₃ — each has at least one lone pair.
**Electrophiles** (electron-pair acceptors): H⁺, R₃C⁺ (carbocations), and δ+ atoms such as the carbon in Cᵟ⁺–Brᵟ⁻ or the polarised Br in Br₂ approaching a double bond.
A reaction happens when the nucleophile's electrons reach the electrophile's electron-poor site.
The same molecule can behave differently in different contexts — H₂O is a nucleophile here and a Lewis base in acid–base chemistry.

sections.content: Family 1 — Nucleophilic substitution of halogenoalkanes

A halogenoalkane has a polar C–X bond (X = Cl, Br, I): because the halogen is more electronegative, the carbon carries a partial positive charge, Cᵟ⁺–Xᵟ⁻. That δ+ carbon is an electrophilic centre, and a nucleophile is attracted to it. When the nucleophile forms a bond to the carbon, the halogen leaves as a halide ion (the leaving group). The nucleophile has SUBSTITUTED for the halogen — hence nucleophilic substitution. With hydroxide ion as the nucleophile the product is an alcohol; the overall equation for bromoethane is:
CH_3CH_2Br + OH^- \rightarrow CH_3CH_2OH + Br^-

Read the equation as a story: the lone pair on OH⁻ attacks the δ+ carbon bonded to Br, a new C–O bond forms, and the C–Br bond breaks with both electrons leaving on the bromine to give Br⁻. The general pattern, for any nucleophile Nu⁻ and halogenoalkane R–X, is R–X + Nu⁻ → R–Nu + X⁻. Different nucleophiles give different products from the same halogenoalkane: OH⁻ gives an alcohol, CN⁻ gives a nitrile (adding a carbon to the chain), and NH₃ gives an amine.

Two things earn easy marks and are easy to lose: (1) name the target — the nucleophile attacks the carbon bonded to the halogen, the δ+ carbon, NOT the halogen itself; (2) show the halide as a separate ion (Br⁻), not still attached. Stating the reaction type in the exact words 'nucleophilic substitution' is usually worth its own mark, so write it in full.

sections.question: 1-bromopropane reacts with warm aqueous sodium hydroxide. (a) State the type of reaction. (b) Write the overall equation. (c) Identify the nucleophile and the atom it attacks. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a)** The reaction is **nucleophilic substitution**: a nucleophile replaces the halogen on a saturated carbon.  [A1]

**(b)** Hydroxide replaces bromine to give propan-1-ol and a bromide ion:
$CH_3CH_2CH_2Br + OH^- \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2OH + Br^-$.  [A1: correct organic product AND balanced equation]

*(Written with the full reagent, $CH_3CH_2CH_2Br + NaOH \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2OH + NaBr$, is equally acceptable — Na⁺ is a spectator.)*

**(c)** The **nucleophile is the hydroxide ion, OH⁻** (it donates a lone pair from oxygen).  [A1]
It attacks the **δ+ carbon — the carbon atom bonded to the bromine** — because the polar C–Br bond leaves that carbon electron-deficient.  [A1: reasoning about δ+ carbon]

*Note the two ideas the marks separate: correctly NAMING the nucleophile, and correctly identifying WHERE it attacks and why.*

sections.content: HL — how the substitution actually happens: SN1 vs SN2

SL stops at the overall equation. At HL you also need the mechanism, and there are two, distinguished by how many species take part in the rate-determining step. In **SN2** (substitution, nucleophilic, bimolecular) the nucleophile attacks the δ+ carbon in the SAME step as the C–X bond breaks — one concerted step, with the leaving group departing on the opposite side. Because two species (the nucleophile and the halogenoalkane) are involved in that single step, the rate depends on both concentrations, and the reaction is fastest for PRIMARY halogenoalkanes, where the small carbon centre is easy to approach. In **SN1** (substitution, nucleophilic, unimolecular) the C–X bond breaks FIRST to give a carbocation intermediate (the slow, rate-determining step), and only then does the nucleophile attack. Only the halogenoalkane is in the rate-determining step, so the rate depends on its concentration alone; this route dominates for TERTIARY halogenoalkanes, whose bulky, branched structure both hinders direct attack and stabilises the tertiary carbocation.

items: **SN2** — one concerted step; rate depends on [halogenoalkane] AND [nucleophile]; favoured by PRIMARY halogenoalkanes (little steric hindrance).
**SN1** — two steps via a carbocation; slow step is C–X breaking; rate depends only on [halogenoalkane]; favoured by TERTIARY halogenoalkanes (stable carbocation).
Secondary halogenoalkanes can react by both routes.
Leaving-group ability: C–I breaks most easily and C–F least, so iodoalkanes react fastest by either mechanism.

sections.content: Family 2 — Electrophilic addition to alkenes

Alkenes have a carbon–carbon double bond, C=C, which is a region of high electron density sitting exposed above and below the carbons. That electron-rich π bond is attractive to electrophiles — species short of electrons. When an electrophile approaches, the double bond opens and adds atoms across the two carbons: one new single bond forms to each carbon, the C=C becomes C–C, and NOTHING leaves. This is electrophilic addition, and because every atom of both reactants ends up in one product, it has 100% atom economy in principle. With bromine, ethene gives 1,2-dibromoethane; with hydrogen bromide it gives bromoethane:
CH_2{=}CH_2 + Br_2 \rightarrow CH_2Br{-}CH_2Br

The reaction of an alkene with bromine (bromine water) is also the classic TEST for a C=C double bond: orange bromine water is decolourised as the addition takes place. With a symmetrical alkene like ethene there is only one possible product. But with an unsymmetrical alkene and an unsymmetrical reagent like HBr, two products are possible depending on which carbon gets the H and which gets the Br — and that is where Markovnikov's rule comes in.

items: Alkene + Br₂ → dibromoalkane (the double bond opens; bromine water goes from orange to colourless — the test for unsaturation).
Alkene + HBr → bromoalkane; alkene + H₂O (acid catalyst) → alcohol.
Addition breaks the C=C π bond and forms two new single bonds; no leaving group, one product.
Contrast with alkanes, which are saturated and undergo radical SUBSTITUTION, not addition.

sections.content: Markovnikov's rule: predicting the major product

When an unsymmetrical molecule such as HBr, HCl or H₂O adds to an unsymmetrical alkene, two products are possible, but one forms in greater amount. Markovnikov's rule predicts it: the hydrogen atom adds to the carbon of the double bond that already carries MORE hydrogen atoms, which puts the halogen (or OH) on the more substituted carbon. So propene, CH₃CH=CH₂, plus HBr gives mainly 2-bromopropane, CH₃CHBrCH₃, not 1-bromopropane. (The HL reason is that adding H⁺ this way forms the more stable — more substituted — carbocation intermediate; at SL you apply the rule to name the major product.)

sections.question: But-1-ene, CH₃CH₂CH=CH₂, reacts with hydrogen bromide, HBr. (a) State the type of reaction. (b) Give the overall equation and the name of the MAJOR organic product, justifying your choice. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a)** **Electrophilic addition** — an electrophile (HBr) adds across the C=C double bond, which opens.  [A1]

**(b)** Two carbons of the double bond can receive the H. The terminal CH₂ carbon already has 2 H atoms; the internal CH carbon has 1 H atom. By **Markovnikov's rule the H adds to the carbon with more H atoms** (the terminal CH₂), so Br goes on the more substituted (internal) carbon.  [A1: correct application of Markovnikov reasoning]

Overall equation:
$CH_3CH_2CH{=}CH_2 + HBr \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CHBrCH_3$.  [A1: correct balanced equation]

Major product: **2-bromobutane**, $CH_3CH_2CHBrCH_3$.  [A1: correct name/structure of major product]

*(1-bromobutane, $CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2Br$, is the minor product — do not give it as the main answer.)*

sections.content: HL — the electrophilic addition mechanism

At HL you show the mechanism with curly arrows. Take HBr adding to an alkene. The H–Br bond is polar (Hᵟ⁺–Brᵟ⁻), so the electron-rich C=C π bond attacks the δ+ hydrogen: a curly arrow runs from the double bond to the H, and a second arrow runs from the H–Br bond to the bromine, which leaves as Br⁻. This makes a carbocation on one carbon (the more stable one, following Markovnikov). In the second step a curly arrow runs from the lone pair on Br⁻ to the positive carbon, forming the C–Br bond and completing the addition. For Br₂, the non-polar Br–Br bond becomes polarised as it approaches the electron-rich double bond (an induced dipole), and the same two-step pattern follows via a bromonium-ion/carbocation intermediate.

items: Step 1: C=C π electrons attack the δ+ atom (H of HBr, or the nearer Br of polarised Br₂); the other bond breaks heterolytically to give a leaving anion and a carbocation. This step controls Markovnikov selectivity.
Step 2: the anion's lone pair attacks the carbocation, forming the second new bond.
Every arrow starts electron-rich (the π bond, or a lone pair) and points electron-poor — the same rule as everywhere else.
The carbocation forms on the carbon that gives the MORE stable (more substituted) cation, which is why Markovnikov's rule works.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Swapping nucleophile and electrophile** — a nucleophile DONATES electrons (electron-rich, e.g. OH⁻); an electrophile ACCEPTS them (electron-poor, e.g. H⁺). Check which one gives and which takes before you label anything.
**Saying the nucleophile attacks the halogen** — it attacks the δ+ CARBON bonded to the halogen; the halogen leaves afterwards as the halide ion.

**Confusing substitution with addition** — halogenoalkanes (saturated, with a leaving group) undergo nucleophilic SUBSTITUTION; alkenes (with a C=C) undergo electrophilic ADDITION. Adding OH across nothing, or making an alkene 'lose' a group, mixes the families up.
**Drawing the curly arrow backwards** — it must go from the electron-rich site (lone pair or bond) to the electron-poor site, never from the positive carbon towards the nucleophile.
**Ignoring Markovnikov's rule** — for HBr + an unsymmetrical alkene, the H goes on the carbon with more H atoms; give the MAJOR product, not the minor one.
**Leaving the equation unbalanced or the halide still attached** — show the leaving group as a free ion (Br⁻) and check every atom balances.

**(HL) Mislabelling the mechanism** — primary halogenoalkanes favour SN2 (one step, rate depends on both species); tertiary favour SN1 (carbocation intermediate, rate depends on the halogenoalkane only). Don't state a rate law that contradicts the mechanism you drew.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

This is the showcase. In Paper 2 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific statement — a method/reasoning mark (M) or an answer/identification mark (A) — and error-carried-forward (ECF) means a wrong earlier step does not automatically cost the marks that depend on it, PROVIDED your reasoning is written down. Study how each of the four marks below is earned by a specific line, and how equivalents are accepted.

sections.question: Bromoethane reacts with aqueous sodium hydroxide. State the type of reaction, give the overall equation, and identify the nucleophile and the atom it attacks. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

Type of reaction: **nucleophilic substitution**.

Overall equation: $CH_3CH_2Br + OH^- \rightarrow CH_3CH_2OH + Br^-$
(equivalently $CH_3CH_2Br + NaOH \rightarrow CH_3CH_2OH + NaBr$).

Nucleophile: the **hydroxide ion, OH⁻**, which donates a lone pair of electrons from its oxygen atom.

Atom attacked: the **δ+ carbon atom — the carbon bonded to the bromine** — because the polar Cᵟ⁺–Brᵟ⁻ bond leaves that carbon electron-deficient.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks (1 each):**

- **A1 — reaction type.** Awarded for 'nucleophilic substitution'. The full two-word term is required; 'substitution' alone may not score, and 'nucleophilic addition' scores zero (wrong family).
- **A1 — correct overall equation / organic product.** Awarded for a balanced equation with the correct organic product, **ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH)**, and the bromide shown as Br⁻. *Accept equivalents:* the molecular form with NaOH/NaBr is fine. This is an ANSWER mark for the correct product and balancing.
- **A1 — nucleophile identified.** Awarded for naming **OH⁻ / the hydroxide ion**. 'NaOH' is accepted as the reagent, but the species donating the electrons is OH⁻.
- **M1 — reasoning: which atom is attacked and why.** Awarded for stating the nucleophile attacks the **δ+ carbon (the carbon bonded to Br)**, with the reasoning that the C–Br bond is polar so that carbon is electron-deficient. This is a METHOD/reasoning mark: it credits the explanation, not just a label. *Do NOT accept* 'attacks the bromine'.

**ECF in action.** If a student wrote the wrong product formula in the equation but then correctly identified OH⁻ as the nucleophile and explained the attack on the δ+ carbon, the two later marks are still awarded on their reasoning — the equation slip is penalised once, not three times. Equally, a student who names the nucleophile as OH⁻ and then says it attacks 'the δ+ carbon' consistently earns the reasoning mark even if their equation was imperfect.

**Bottom line:** four independent marks, each from a specific statement. Writing only 'CH₃CH₂OH' with no type, no nucleophile and no target risks losing 3 of the 4. Spell out the type, the species and the δ+ carbon and you bank each mark separately.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer exactly this way — analytically, statement by statement, with ECF. So write each idea explicitly: name the reaction type in full, give a balanced equation with the halide as a free ion, name the nucleophile as OH⁻, and say it attacks the δ+ carbon bonded to the halogen and why. Each of those is a separate mark, and the reasoning marks survive an equation slip only if the reasoning is actually on the page.
Where this leads

Every named organic reaction you meet from here is a variation on these two moves plus radical substitution. Halogenoalkanes go on to make alcohols, nitriles and amines by nucleophilic substitution; alcohols and alkenes interconvert by addition and elimination; and the curly-arrow habit — start electron-rich, point electron-poor — carries straight into the HL mechanisms and into any new reagent you are asked to reason about. Master 'find the δ+ centre and follow the electron pair' and unfamiliar organic questions become recognisable.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 mechanism question marked: classify the reaction, write the equation, and identify the nucleophile and δ+ carbon with full reasoning
